title: Yemista
subtitle: Greek baked stuffed tomatoes and peppers
excerpt: Tomatoes and peppers hollowed out and filled with herbed rice, then baked slowly until the skins wrinkle and the rice is cooked through. A summer dish built for a hot afternoon.
sourceNotes: Greek household staple documented in Vefa Alexiadou, Greek Cuisine (1991) and Diane Kochilas, The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001). The rice-filling method is consistent across Greek home cooking; variations include adding mince (gemistá me kima) or keeping it purely vegetarian.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Yemista reheat well in a low oven. They also taste good at room temperature, which is how they are often eaten the next day.

content.text: Yemista (from the Greek for 'stuffed') is the dish that defines a Greek summer kitchen: heavy ripe tomatoes and fat peppers, halved and hollowed, filled with rice and herbs and olive oil, then slid into the oven for over an hour. The result is soft, collapsing, perfumed with the juices that have run down into the rice.

The rice goes in raw. It cooks entirely inside the vegetables, absorbing their juice and the olive oil as it steams. This means the rice must be surrounded by liquid — both the juice from the hollowed-out tomatoes poured back in, and a little water in the bottom of the tray. Don't be tempted to pre-cook the rice; a stodgy filling is the usual result.

items.prepNote: cut into wedges, to fill the gaps

content.text: A baking dish large enough to hold all the vegetables upright without too much space between them. Potato wedges tucked in the gaps are optional but traditional — they roast in the olive oil that runs off the vegetables and are usually the first thing eaten.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Slice the tops off the tomatoes and peppers and set the tops aside — they go back on as lids. Use a spoon to scoop out the tomato flesh into a bowl, leaving a shell about 1 cm thick. Finely chop the scooped-out flesh (or blitz briefly) and reserve the juice. Core and deseed the peppers. Season the insides of all the vegetables lightly with salt.

Combine the rice, chopped tomato flesh and juices, onion, garlic, tomato purée, parsley, mint, 60 ml of the olive oil, sugar, salt, and pepper in a bowl. Mix well. The mixture should look quite wet — the rice needs that liquid.

Stand the hollowed vegetables in the baking dish. Fill each three-quarters full with the rice mixture, leaving room for the rice to swell. Replace the tops. Tuck potato wedges in the gaps if using. Pour 150 ml of water into the bottom of the dish. Drizzle the remaining 20 ml of olive oil over the tops. Cover tightly with foil and bake for 50 minutes, then remove the foil and bake for a further 20 minutes until the skins colour and the rice is cooked through. Check by cutting into one dolma — the rice should be tender, not chalky.

items.symptom: Rice is still undercooked after 70 minutes
items.cause: Too little liquid in the filling or the oven temperature was too low
items.fix: Add a splash of water to the baking dish, re-cover with foil, and bake for another 20 minutes

items.symptom: Vegetables collapse before the rice is cooked
items.cause: Very ripe tomatoes can collapse early in a hot oven
items.fix: Use firmer tomatoes or reduce the oven temperature to 160°C fan and extend the cooking time

items.cause: Not enough tomato juice or water added to the tray
items.fix: Use the full amount of tomato flesh and juice; add more water to the base of the tray next time

items.cause: Cut too thick or not enough oil around them
items.fix: Cut wedges no thicker than 2 cm and make sure they sit in the olive oil that drains from the vegetables

content.text: Yemista appears on Greek tables from late June until the end of the tomato season. In villages, the baking tray goes to the communal oven early in the morning and sits there all day on a low heat. The version with a little lamb mince added to the filling is called gemistá me kima and is closer to a main course. The pure-rice version is typically vegetarian, though it is rarely labelled as such — it simply is what it is.
